coredata.dc:title: Enhanced cathodic electrochemiluminescent microcystin-LR aptasensor based on surface plasmon resonance of Bi nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Ruishuang
Liu, Qian
Hong, Honghong
Ma, Hanyu
Xiao, Liting
Li, Yaqi
Jiang, Ding
Hao, Nan
Wang, Kun
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive testing for Microcystins-LR (MC-LR) is needed because of its serious environmental and human health hazards. In this work, a new type of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) enhanced cathodic electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensing platform was designed in which boron and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (BN-GQDs) were used as the luminary and bismuth nanoparticles (Bi NPs) were used as the SPR source. SPR effect of non-precious metal Bi NPs can induce and enhance ECL signal of BN-GQDs because the fluorescence spectrum of BN-GQDs overlaps well with the ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum of Bi NPs. On this basis, a sensitive sensing system based on the Bi NPs and BN-GQDs was established for MC-LR detection. The results showed that the ECL sensing signal obtained was linear with the negative logarithm of the target MC-LR concentration in the range of 0.01–5000 pM, and the detection limit was 0.003 pM. In addition, the sensor had high stability and good reproducibility, which can be applied to the detection of MC-LR in actual samples. The method had good specificity and can not be disturbed by its homolog, which can be used for sensitive and reliable detection of complex samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi NPs
SPR-enhanced ECL
BN-GQDs
Aptasensor
MC-LR

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the BN-GQDs and Bi NPs
Mechanism study of the ECL aptasensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key R&D Program of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial support from Key R&D Program of Jiangsu Province (No. BE2020677 ); National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21675066 , 22174055 and 31901799 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M692370 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of Bi NPs and (B) TEM image of BN-GQDs. XRD patterns of (C) Bi NPs and (D) BN-GQDs.

(A) The ﬂuorescence maximum excitation and emission spectra of BN-GQDs (luminescence diagram under ultraviolet irradiation). (B) Full-scan XPS spectrum, high-resolution (C) B 1 s and (D) N 1 s spectra of the BN-GQDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of DNA2 (a), BN-GQDs (b), and BN-GQDs-DNA2 (c). (B) The fluorescence spectrum of BN-GQDs (a) and the UV−vis absorption spectrum of Bi NPs (b).
(A) CVs and (B) Nyquist plots of (a) bare GCE, (b) Bi/GCE, (c) aptamer/DNA1/Bi/GCE, (d) BN-GQDs-DNA2/aptamer/DNA1/Bi/GCE in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (insert: circuit diagram of EIS).

(A) CVs and (B) ECL performances of (a) bare GCE, (b) Bi/GCE, (c) BN-GQDs-DNA2/aptamer/DNA1/GCE, and (d) BN-GQDs-DNA2/aptamer/DNA1 /Bi/GCE in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) solution with 0.05 M K2S2O8. (C) Nyquist plots of (a) aptamer/DNA1/Bi/GCE, (b) BN-GQDs-DNA2/aptamer/DNA1/Bi/GCE, and (c) MC-LR/BN-GQDs-DNA2/aptamer/DNA1/Bi/ GCE in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl. (D) ECL performance of (a) aptamer/DNA1/Bi/GCE, (b) BN-GQDs-DNA2/aptamer/DNA1/Bi/GCE, and (c) MC-LR/BN-GQDs-DNA2/ aptamer/DNA1/Bi/GCE in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) solution with 0.05 M K2S2O8.

(A) ECL performances of the prepared aptasensor in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) solution containing 0.05 M K2S2O8 after MC-LR being incubated with 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, and 5000 pM (a-l). (B) The calibration curves for the detection of MC-LR. (C) The selectivity of prepared MC-LR aptasensor. (D) ECL performance of BN-GQDs-DNA2/aptamer/DNA1/Bi/GCE in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) solution with 0.05 M K2S2O8 for 10 consecutive cycles.
Schematic diagram of the sensor for MC-LR detection based on Bi NPs and BN-GQDs surface plasmon resonance.
Comparison the results between this method and other reported methods for the determination of MC-LR.
The MC-LR detection and recovery in lake water and tap water.

head.ce:title: Enhanced cathodic electrochemiluminescent microcystin-LR aptasensor based on surface plasmon resonance of Bi nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213164, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213164, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive testing for Microcystins-LR (MC-LR) is needed because of its serious environmental and human health hazards. In this work, a new type of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) enhanced cathodic electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensing platform was designed in which boron and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (BN-GQDs) were used as the luminary and bismuth nanoparticles (Bi NPs) were used as the SPR source. SPR effect of non-precious metal Bi NPs can induce and enhance ECL signal of BN-GQDs because the fluorescence spectrum of BN-GQDs overlaps well with the ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum of Bi NPs. On this basis, a sensitive sensing system based on the Bi NPs and BN-GQDs was established for MC-LR detection. The results showed that the ECL sensing signal obtained was linear with the negative logarithm of the target MC-LR concentration in the range of 0.01–5000 pM, and the detection limit was 0.003 pM. In addition, the sensor had high stability and good reproducibility, which can be applied to the detection of MC-LR in actual samples. The method had good specificity and can not be disturbed by its homolog, which can be used for sensitive and reliable detection of complex samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Microcystins is a secondary metabolite of eutrophication intensifies, which can be detected in algal blooms (Bortoli and Volmer, 2014). It was reported more than 90 homoloids have been identified and the microcystin-LR (MC-LR) is most frequently mentioned due to its abundance and potential toxicity (Kordasht et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2021). The MC-LR which can lead to disease and death after absorbed by human body are mainly sourced from the consumption of seafood, agricultural plants and drinking water (Gaget et al., 2017; Rezaitabar et al., 2017). MC-LR was classified as a possible human carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (Zegura et al., 2011). At the same time, the World Health Organization has set the threshold of MC-LR in drinking water at 1 µg/L as an acceptable level (Shi et al., 2012). Due to the toxicity at low concentration, absorption by the easy way and variety with amount in the environment, it is urgent to develop a sensitive analysis method for MC-LR detection (Zanato et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017). Various analytical methods have been used for MC-LR determination such as high performance liquid chromatography, protein phosphatase inhibition test and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Jin et al., 2021; Sedan et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021), while there are some shortcoming of complex experiment process, expensive instruments and professional operation (Wang, 2017; Wei et al., 2017). Therefore, a simple and fast method is urgently to be fabricated to detect MC-LR with the high sensitivity and excellent selectivity.

Cathodic electrochemiluminescence (ECL) as a super-sensitive detection method with the advantages of the simplicity, high sensitivity, low background interference, and a wide linear range is employed for determination in different field (Zhou et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020). Especially, it was applied to prolongating the count of ECL reactions, clearing off the byproducts of luminescence groups and eliminating the distraction of matters existing to achieve the higher sensitivity (Zhang et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2021; Peng et al., 2020). The selection of luminary is crucial in ECL detection, and Ru(bpy)3 2+ used as the luminary is one of the most classical electrochemiluminescence systems with a wide range of applications (Sui et al., 2020), it is still worth considering its expensive price and optical properties with difficult of regulating, which is hard to meet the development needs of low cost and optical stability in ECL analysis (Chen et al., 2011; Shang et al., 2021). As new luminescent materials, carbon-based doped materials have superior water solubility, positive stability, favorable fluorescence regulation and pleasurable optical stability, which shows promising application prospect in the field of cathodic ECL analysis (Yuan et al., 2021; Dong et al., 2012). Among the various carbon-based doped materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted the attention of many researchers due to their good biocompatibility, water-solubility, fluorescence regulation and light stability (Chen et al., 2014). In addition, GQDs have more important application value in the field of biological imaging and sensor due to its advantages such as low biotoxicity, good stability and light corrosion resistance (Lin et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2021). In recent years, researchers have greatly changed the electronic properties of GQDs through doping of non-metallic elements, thereby realizing the adjustment of the optical properties of GQDs (Qian et al., 2020; You et al., 2020). Among them, the hybridization of boron and nitrogen atoms into the GQD structure not only increases the electronegativity strength, can effectively generate more electron active sites, but also produces a complementary coupling effect between heteroatoms which have excellent performance and broad application prospects in the fields of biological cells and optoelectronic devices (Yuan et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2021). So boron and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (BN-GQDs) as a kind of carbon-based doped luminescent materials, which has attracted much attention in various fields due to their excellent luminescence properties (Yuan et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020).

In order to further enhance ECL signal, surface plasmon resonance (SPR) induction-enhanced ECL technology as an effective enhancement strategy has attracted wide attention. In the significantly enhanced ECL sensing designed by the SPR of noble metal nanoparticles, the ECL emission of quantum dots can induce the SPR of noble metal nanoparticles, which in turn can enhance the ECL response of quantum dots (Liu et al., 2018). Therefore, the introduction of plasmon nanomaterials with SPR effect can further induce and enhance the ECL signal, and has been widely applied in biological detection and material science (Liu et al., 2018; Feng et al., 2017). Studies have shown that noble metal nanomaterials have SPR effect in the procedure of sensor development, while the properties of weak storage stability and high cost exhibited some drawback of practical application in plasma-enhanced ECL sensors (Z. Liu et al., 2020; Y. Liu et al., 2020). Non-precious metal bismuth nanoparticles (Bi NPs) have been found to possess the impression which is similar to the noble metal nanomaterials including Au and Ag to achieve SPR. Furthermore, this characteristic can be used to promote charge separation and transport electrons (R. Zhang et al., 2018; X.L. Zhang et al., 2018). At the same time, Bi is well conducive to the ECL luminescence reaction due to its high hydrogen over-potential (Hu et al., 2007). The SPR effect of Bi NPs was used to replace noble metal gold to design a significantly enhanced ECL with SPR effect to help monitor the interface processes involved in ECL with better performance (Dinel et al., 2019).

In this work, non-noble metal Bi NPs enhanced ECL biosensor based on BN-GQDs was designed for MC-LR detection. The preparation scheme and mechanism of the biosensor are shown in Scheme 1, the non noble metal Bi NPs replaces the traditional noble metal as the resonance source, and uses the green and environmentally friendly BN-GQDs with high ECL efficiency as the luminescent body, the DNA strand can realize SPR and resonance energy transfer. The ECL emission of BN-GQDs can induce the SPR of Bi NPs, and the SPR induced by Bi NPs in turn can enhance the ECL response of BN-GQDs, so as to enhance the ECL signal and the new SPR enhanced ECL aptasensor can be used for MC-LR detection. And compared with our previous work of ZnO QDs loaded on boron and nitrogen co-doped graphene as the luminary (Yuan et al., 2021), the “on-off” type super-sensitive sensor based on SPR of non noble metal Bi NPs and ECL of BN-GQDs was beneficial to broaden the detection range, lower detection limit and has more potential application prospects in biological analysis.

The MC-LR aptamer (5′-GGC GCC AAA CAG GAC CAC CAT GAC AAT TAC CCA TAC CAC CTC ATT ATG CCC CAT CTC CGC-3′) (Du et al., 2020), DNA1 (5′-CTG TTT GGC GCC-NH2-3′) and DNA2 (5′-NH2-GCG GAG ATG GGG-3′) were chemically synthesized and purified by Shenggong Bioengineering Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bi NPs were prepared based on Xu′s work (Xu et al., 2020). BN-GQDs were fabricated based on Liu′s work (Y. Liu et al., 2020; Z. Liu et al., 2020). All reagents used are of analytical grade and listed in the supporting information.

3 mL prepared BN-GQDs, 50 μL 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)− 3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (200 µM) and 50 μL N-hydroxysuccinimide (200 µM) were added to the centrifuge tube and shaken for 2 h to activate the carboxyl groups at BN-GQDs to prepare BN-GQDs-labeled nano-biological probes. Then 20 μL of DNA2 (100 µM) was added to the above solution and stirred for 24 h to obtain the BN-GQDs-labeled nano-biological probe BN-GQDs-DNA2, and the DNA2 without bonding was removed by centrifugal washing for several times.

Before modification, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished to a mirror-like condition successively with 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05 mm aluminum oxide slurry, then thoroughly rinsed with distilled water, absolute ethanol, and distilled water in turn. Following, the cleaned working electrode was blow-dried with N2 at room temperature. 10 μL Bi NPs (2 mg/mL) as the SPR source were dropped on a GCE, and then dried with N2. 5 μL of chitosan (0.1%) was added as the fixing agent to the surface of the electrode. After the electrode was dried, 10 μL of glutaraldehyde (2.5%) was added and reacted with the electrode at room temperature for 1 h (Xu et al., 2020). Then 5 μL aminated DNA1 (8 µM) and 5 μL aptamer (8 µM) were added to the electrode for modification, and after incubating for 4 h, they were rinsed with PBS and dried. After that, 10 μL BN-GQDs-DNA2 as the luminary was dropped onto the electrode and reacted for 4 h to bound with aptamer, and the SPR enhanced cathodic ECL aptasensing platform was designed. For the detection of MC-LR, 5 μL the target was then added and incubated for some time to combine with the aptamer and separated from DNA. The preparation scheme and mechanism of the biosensor are shown in Scheme 1. Based on this principle, MC-LR can be quantitatively detected. The ECL signals of the aptasensor were collected and recorded in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) solution containing 0.05 M K2S2O8 with 800 V photomultiplier tube voltage and the scanning potential was from − 2.0–0 V. The whole system was tested at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the BN-GQDs and Bi NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The images from scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) showed the morphology characterization of the materials. Morphology and microstructure of BN-GQDs and Bi NPs were observed. Fig. 1 A showed that the synthesized Bi NPs were approximately round spheres with a particle size of about 100 nm. The morphology of BN-GQDs (Fig. 1B) was approximately spherical, with uniform distribution and particle size distribution rang was from 10 nm to 13 nm. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Bi NPs (Fig. 1 C) was consistent with the standard card JCPDS 44–1246 of Bi (Xu et al., 2020), indicating that the prepared material was Bi NPs. Fig. 1D showed that there was a diffraction peak at 13.3° in the figure that corresponds to graphene oxide (GO) (002) (Lü et al., 2016), and the diffraction peak at 27.5° corresponded to the in-plane characteristics of graphene (Y. Liu et al., 2020; Z. Liu et al., 2020), which showed that this substance was BN-GQDs and BN-GQDs have the both characteristics of GO and graphene. XRD patterns proved the successful synthesis of Bi and BN-GQDs.

Fig. 2 A was the fluorescence spectrum of BN-GQDs, including the maximum excitation wavelength at 322 nm, the maximum emission wavelength at 422 nm, and it showed blue light under ultraviolet light. The result above proved the successful synthesis of BN-GQDs with fluorescence. Fig. 2B was a full scan of the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum showing the chemical composition of BN-GQDs (containing B, N, C and O). From the Fig. 2 C, three forms of boron (191.5 eV, 192.4 eV and 193.4 eV) were found belonging to B-N, B-O and B-C groups, respectively (Tran et al., 2020). Fig. 2D showed that we observed that N 1 s could be divided into four peaks at 399.9 eV, 398.1 eV, 401.3 eV and 402.8 eV, corresponding to pyrrole N, pyridine N, graphite N and oxidized N respectively (Tran et al., 2020). The results of XPS proved the element composition and corresponding chemical bonds in BN-GQDs, and proved the successful preparation of BN-GQDs.

To verify the assembly of BN-GQDs and DNA2, ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy was used to detect the obtained DNA2, BN-GQDs and BN-GQDs-DNA2. Fig. 3 A showed that DNA2 had an absorbance peak at 255 nm (curve a), which corresponded to the characteristic peak of DNA (Liu et al., 2016). It can be seen from curve b that the characteristic absorption peaks of BN-GQDs were detected at about 228 nm and 301 nm (Y. Liu et al., 2020; Z. Liu et al., 2020). The absorption spectrum of BN-GQDs-DNA2 probes appeared the characteristic peak of both DNA2 and BN-GQDs (curve c), which indicated that DNA2 was successfully loaded on the surface of BN-GQDs and the probe was successfully prepared. The ECL spectrum of BN-GQDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Bi NPs were tested to verify whether the plasma resonance phenomenon could be realized. Fig. 3B showed that the ECL emission peak of BN-GQDs (curve a) and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Bi NPs (curve b) had a good overlap, indicating that the emission spectrum of the BN-GQDs matched well with the absorption spectrum of the Bi NPs and the plasmon resonance can be achieved to enhance ECL signal (Feng et al., 2019), it is proved that the construction of the sensor is feasible.

Cyclic voltammograms (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were used to characterize the electrode surface assembly process. Fig. 4A showed that the bare electrode had a clear redox peak of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. The redox peak current of Bi/GCE was decreased because surfactants were added in the synthesis of Bi NPs, so the existence of Bi NPs affected the electron transfer on the electrode (curve b). With the addition of aptamer and DNA1, the peak current was further reduced (curve c), this was because the contact of the redox probe was hindered by DNA. When BN-GQDs-DNA2 was combined with aptamer, it was assembled into a double strand on the modified electrode, which further hindered the reaction of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (curve d) and reduced the redox current of the electrode. The result of the current change shows that the electrode has been successfully constructed. These results of EIS (Fig. 4B) further verified the electrode assembly process. A small semicircle appeared on the bare GCE (curve a), while the Ret of Bi/GCE increased (curve b). After the assembly of aptamer and DNA1, the electron transfer resistance increased (curve c), this was because the phosphorous backbone of the negatively charged oligonucleotide inhibited the approach of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. After adding BN-GQDs-DNA2 to the modified electrode (curve d), Ret further increased which because the electron transfer at the electrode surface was hindered, which was consistent with the results of CVs. All these results above indicated the successful preparation of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism study of the ECL aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The ECL mechanism of BN-GQDs and K2S2O8 was discussed and CV and ECL experiments of different modified electrodes were carried out in PBS with and without K2S2O8. The reduction peak can be observed on the bare electrode ( Fig. 5 A, curve a), while there was no peak in the bare electrode in PBS (Fig. S1A, curve a), which was matched the reduction peak of K2S2O8 (R. Zhang et al., 2018; X.L. Zhang et al., 2018). The modification of Bi NPs obstructed the electron transfer on the electrode surface and reduces the current signal because surfactants were added in the synthesis of Bi NPs. The reduction peak potential of K2S2O8 moved to a more negative direction at about 1.5 V when BN-GQDs were modified on the electrode. The shift of peak potential was attributed to the reaction between BN-GQDs and K2S2O8 (Wang et al., 2011). Meanwhile, the presence of Bi reduced the reduction peak current of curve d, but the peak potential didn’t change significantly. The figure also proved by the detection of ECL that in the absence of K2S2O8, no ECL signal appeared in the curve a (Fig. S1B). Fig. 5B showed a weak ECL signal on curve a, which may be related to the emission of singlet oxygen (1(O2)2 * ) in the S2O8 2- solution (Koval'chuk et al., 1999). With the modification of Bi NPs, the ECL signal was no significant change (curve b). Due to modification of BN-GQDs, curve c showed an obvious ECL signal while BN-GQDs have no ECL signal in PBS (Fig. S1B, curve c), which was contributed to the enhancement of the luminescence of BN-GQDs of the product of K2S2O8 electrochemical reduction (Wang et al., 2011). Furthermore, compared with the ECL response of curve c, the ECL intensity of curve d displayed enhanced ECL responses about 1.6 times. Electrodes with and without Bi NPs showed almost the same profile (Fig. S2), but the ECL signal of the electrode was significantly enhanced, implying that the surface plasmon effect of Bi NPs enhanced the ECL signal of BN-GQDs (Rekeb et al., 2017). In the ECL system, BN-GQDs and K2S2O8 were reduced to form BN-GQDs·- and SO4 ·-, and then oxidized by SO4 ·- to form BN-GQDs* , which obtained ECL strength from the excited state to the ground state. The entire ECL process can be described as follows (X.L. Zhang et al., 2018; R. Zhang et al., 2018):
BN-GQDs + e- → BN-GQDs·­
S2O8 2- + e- → SO4 2- + SO4 ·­
BN-GQDs·­ + SO4 ·­ → BN-GQDs* + SO4 2-
BN-GQDs* → BN-GQDs + hν

Fig. 5 C depicted the production process of the ECL aptasensor. As can be seen, the aptamer/DNA1/Bi showed the lowest electrochemical impedance (curve a). After the modification of BN-GQDs-DNA2 (curve b), the impedance value on the electrode surface increased due to the limitation of electron transfer. When MC-LR was incubated, the binding of toxin and aptamer hindered the signal conduction between Bi NPs and BN-GQDs, which reduced the electron transfer barrier and reduced the impedance value (curve c) (Yuan et al., 2021). The EIS results showed that the successful construction of the sensor. In addition, as can be seen from Fig. 5D, the ECL signal was weak when there was no BN-GQDs (curve a). The curve b showed the strongest ECL signal when the aptamer binds to BN-GQDs-DNA2, which was because the DNA connects BN-GQDs and Bi NPs to produce plasma resonance. When target was recognized with the corresponding aptamer, MC-LR would combine its aptamer specifically thus cutting off the connection between BN-GQDs and Bi NPs, and further weakening the ECL performance (curve c) proving that the detection of MC-LR could be come true.

The experimental temperature, DNA1 concentration, the incubation time of DNA1, aptamer concentration, the reaction time of BN-GQDs-DNA2 and MC-LR incubation time were optimized in the experiment to optimize the performance of the sensor. Considering the influence of temperature on DNA chain, we chose four temperatures to optimize it. The results showed that DNA chain can better combine with the sensing interface at 37 °C, and high or low temperature will affect the activity of DNA chain and affect its combination with material (Fig. S3A). The concentration of DNA1 was one of the important factors affecting the strength of the constructed ECL. Low concentration of DNA1 will reduce the binding of aptamer, and was not conducive to the construction of the SPR system and MC-LR detection. It can be seen from Fig. S3B that the intensity of the constructed ECL gradually decreased as the concentration of DNA1 increased. When the concentration of DNA1 reached 8 μM, the ECL intensity tended to be flat and reached a level, which indicates that the load of DNA1 on the sensing interface has reached the maximum. Therefore, 8 μM was selected as the optimal DNA1 concentration in this experiment. At the same time, in order to shorten the reaction time, the incubation time of DNA1 was optimized, and at 4 h the detection signal reaches a relatively stable state (Fig. S3C). Since the aptamer is bound to DNA1, we believe that the concentration of the aptamer and DNA1 should be consistent. According to the optimization results, the maximum binding value is indeed reached at 8 μM, the result was consistent with the optimal concentration of DNA1 (Fig. S3D). And if BN-GQDs-DNA2 was sufficient, considering the influence of the reaction time of BN-GQDs-DNA2 on the sensing system, the binding time was also optimized. Finally, it is concluded that the sensing system constructed in 4 h tends to be stable (Fig. S3E). In addition, the incubation time of MC-LR was also one of the important factors that affect the performance of the sensor. Fig. S3F showed the ECL intensity of the sensor under different incubation times. As the incubation time increased, the ECL intensity of the sensor decreased. When the incubation time reached 35 min, the ECL strength of the sensor reached a plateau, indicating that the hybridization of MC-LR to the aptamer achieved equilibrium. Therefore, it was concluded that the best incubation time was 35 min

The prepared aptasensor was used for the sensitive determination of MC-LR under optimum conditions. Fig. 6 A showed that the ECL signal gradually weakens when the concentration of MC-LR was in the range of 0.001–5000 pM, while the linear range of detection is 0.01–5000 pM. Fig. 6B showed that the linear relationship between MC-LR concentration and the ECL performance and the logarithm of the MC-LR concentration is directly proportional to ECL performance. The regression equation for MC-LR can be depicted as ECL Intensity= 3151.93–625.83 ×log C [pM] (R2 =0.99) with a detection limit of 0.003 pM (S/N = 3). The prepared ECL aptasensor showed a lower detection limit than the previous MC-LR assay method ( Table 1).

The selectivity of prepared MC-LR aptasensor was investigated by assessing the impacts of the 100-fold distraction of homologs and other poisons as well as other inorganic ions that may coexist in the water. The selectivity of the sensor prepared was reflected by analyzing the value of ΔRelative ECL Intensity (ΔRelative ECL Intensity= the ECL Intensity of MC-LR/the ECL Intensity of others). As depicted in Fig. 6 C, the ΔRelative ECL Intensity of the homologues, other poisons or other inorganic ions that may coexist in water was relatively low and significant ECL changes were observed only in the presence of MC-LR, which proved that the sensor with good selectivity will not be interfered by the distraction of homologs, other poisons and other inorganic ions. In addition, Fig. 6D recorded the ECL intensity of the constructed aptasensor after 10 consecutive scans. The ECL signal had no obvious change under continuous scanning and the ECL signal remained 98.3%, which indicated that the as-prepared aptasensor was quite stable. In addition, the ECL signal of aptasensor remained at 94.7% after 2 weeks of storage at 4 °C, indicating that the prepared aptasensor had good long-term stability. Moreover, the relative standard deviation of the ECL signal obtained by the same batch of newly prepared electrode for MC-LR detection was 5.1%, indicating an acceptable reproducibility.

Water samples from lake and tap water were collected and determined by the standard addition method to assess the practicality of the developed ECL aptasensor ( Table 2). The lake water was taken from the small lake beside the library of our university, and the tap water was taken from the laboratory of our research group. The results indicated that the recovery was 96.5%− 102.4% with the RSD of 3.12%− 8.6%, it showed that the MC-LR was decomposed or degraded by cation which affected the recognition of the aptamer and MC-LR (Yuan et al., 2021), and the recovery of the tap water sample was lower than the adding, and the high recovery rate of lake water was the result of the water eutrophication. The results showed that the sensor could be applied to analyze the actual water samples of MC-LR.

In summary, we prepared an SPR enhanced ECL aptasensor based on BN-GQDs as ECL emitters and Bi NPs as SPR source, which can be used for the sensitive and selective detection of MC-LR. As a non noble metal particle, Bi can replace precious metal with SPR characteristics. The SPR effect of Bi can realize signal amplification by the ECL performance of BN-GQDs, so the ECL intensity of BN-GQDs with Bi was significantly higher than that of BN-GQDs without Bi (about 1.6 times). The ECL aptasensor had good specificity and can sensitivity detect MC-LR with a wider linear range and the lower detection limit. At the same time, the analysis results of the actual sample showed that the prepared ECL aptasensor had potential application prospects in practical water analysis. Moreover, the sensor used the complementary pairing of aptamer and DNA base for biometrics, which makes the sensor have a good application in the field of biometrics and detection.

Ruishuang Yuan: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Qian Liu: Experimental supervision and Guidance. Honghong Hong: Data curation. Hanyu Ma: Experimental support. Liting Xiao: Formal analysis. Yaqi Li: Writing – review & editing, Review. Ding Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Review. Nan Hao: Writing – review & editing, Review. Kun Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

MC-LR was considered as a probable human carcinogen, the monitoring of MC-LR is of vital importance in the fields including environment, food, and public health. This work reports an “on-off” SPR enhanced ECL aptasensing model for sensitive detection of MC-LR. Bi NPs as the SPR source had the effect of inducing enhancement of ECL signal and BN-GQDs as the luminous body had excellent ECL performance. Enhanced ECL performance is benefit for the detection sensor which has good specificity and can not be disturbed by its homolog, that can be used for sensitive and reliable detection of complex samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from Key R&D Program of Jiangsu Province (No. BE2020677); National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21675066, 22174055 and 31901799), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M692370).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.128877.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enzyme-free molecularly imprinted and graphene-functionalized photoelectrochemical sensor platform for pollutants
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Song, H. Sun, J. Yu, Y. Wang, M. Li, M. Liu, G. Zhao, Enzyme-Free Molecularly Imprinted and Graphene-Functionalized Photoelectrochemical Sensor Platform for Pollutants, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 13 (2021) 37212–37222.
